name: tier-cap-violation-over-explicit-allow
description: R15 ratification: a sensitive-tier tool has a source: "user" config entry with explicitAllow: true (which, alone, would allow via explicit_config_allow), but a covering grant is ALSO present whose own tierCap is "routine" — below the resolved tier. That grant is an active self-escalation attempt (someone/something offered a grant claiming less authority than the action requires), and tier_cap_violation is checked before the engine ever asks whether an explicit-allow fall-through exists, so it wins outright: a decisive, loud deny at layer 3, even though the config alone would have allowed. This is deliberate fail-closed behavior — an anomalous grant is surfaced as tier_cap_violation, never silently papered over by a legitimate-looking config allow. Contrast with the no-grant baseline under the identical tierPolicy (packages/core/src/precedence.test.ts), which has no grant to raise the anomaly at all and resolves allow/explicit_config_allow at layer 4 — proving the grant's mere presence, not the config, is what flips the outcome.
